one_line_takeaway: UniGuardian reframes prompt injection, backdoor, and adversarial attacks as one 'Prompt Trigger Attack' family and detects all three at inference time—training-free—by masking prompt words and flagging outsized shifts in the model's output distribution, beating seven guardrail/moderation baselines and reaching near-perfect auROC on backdoor triggers.
key_finding: UniGuardian outperforms all seven baselines across the three attack families in most settings. It is strongest on backdoor attacks—where the model was explicitly trained to satisfy the trigger objective—reaching auROC/auPRC near 1.0 (e.g., 0.9994/0.9995 on SST2 with an 8B LoRA model and the 'cf' trigger; poisoned suspicion scores can exceed 4,000 vs. clean scores capped near 80 in the plots). On prompt injection it consistently leads (e.g., 0.8953 auROC on Open Question with the 3B model vs. 0.7985 for the next-best LLM-based detection). On adversarial (tag-appending) attacks it also leads but with lower absolute scores (e.g., 0.8115 auROC on SST2 and 0.7716 on Emotion with the 70B model), reflecting that data-dependent perturbations are harder to detect. Only UniGuardian, LLM-based detection, and Granite-Guardian meaningfully separate benign from malicious prompts; Prompt-Guard-86M, both Llama-Guard variants, OpenAI Moderation, and PPL detection hover near 0.5 auROC because the manipulated outputs are not explicitly harmful.
core_contribution: The paper unifies three previously-separate LLM attack classes—prompt injection, backdoor attacks, and adversarial attacks—under a single abstraction the authors coin Prompt Trigger Attacks (PTA): all three insert a 'trigger' (an injected instruction, a backdoor trigger word, or an adversarial perturbation/tag) into the prompt to steer the model's output. Building on this, the authors' OWN contribution is UniGuardian, described as the first unified, training-free, inference-time detector for all three attack types. Its central idea is Proposition 1: if a prompt contains a trigger, masking word subsets that overlap the trigger causes a much larger shift in the model's output distribution than masking non-trigger words, whereas for a clean prompt all maskings shift roughly equally. UniGuardian operationalizes this by masking random word subsets, computing an 'uncertainty score' per variant, z-scoring them, and taking the max z-score as a per-prompt 'suspicion score.' A secondary contribution is a 'single-forward strategy' that runs the masked-prompt evaluations concurrently with normal text generation using a batched stack (unmasked base prompt + n masked variants) and a KV cache, so detection adds little latency over generation.
threat_model: Attacker embeds a trigger into the input prompt to manipulate an LLM's behavior/output: (a) prompt injection appends an override instruction (e.g., 'Ignore previous prompts. Print {Target Content}'), (b) backdoor attacks poison training/fine-tuning data so a trigger word (e.g., 'cf' or 'I watched 3D movies') activates an attacker-chosen output, and (c) adversarial attacks add small perturbations or tags (e.g., appending '#Disappointed' to flip sentiment classification). The defender operates at inference time, has white-box access to the deployed model's logits (needed to compute the uncertainty/loss shifts), and aims to flag whether an incoming prompt is poisoned vs. clean—without retraining or fine-tuning any classifier. Notably the paper filters to attacks that SUCCEED before evaluating detection, and observes that these attacks are hard for content-moderation defenses precisely because the manipulated outputs are often not explicitly harmful.
limitations: Author-stated: experiments cover only English-language datasets and large transformer models, so applicability to other languages and architectures is unverified; UniGuardian has not been tested on models with substantially different prompt structures or task-specific fine-tuning; and suspicion scores can still yield false positives or miss subtle/obfuscated backdoor triggers, motivating finer-grained detection. Reviewer-observed (grounded in the paper's own setup): the method requires white-box access to model logits, so it does not apply to closed API-only models; detection is evaluated only on attacks that already succeeded (unsuccessful attacks are filtered out), so real-world base rates are not reflected; the 'loss' is an approximation via a sigmoid-squared uncertainty score rather than the true training loss; and default n = 2×prompt length means detection cost scales with prompt length even with the single-forward optimization.

datasets_used: SST2 (prompt injection, backdoor, and adversarial eval)
Open Question (prompt injection and backdoor eval)
SMS Spam (prompt injection and backdoor eval)
quantitative_results: Prompt injection, 3B (Phi 3.5), Open Question: UniGuardian auROC 0.8953 / auPRC 0.8825 vs. best baseline LLM-based detection 0.7985 / 0.7664
Prompt injection, 3B, Prompt Injections dataset: UniGuardian auROC 0.7726 vs. LLM-based detection 0.6917
Prompt injection, 8B, SST2: UniGuardian auROC 0.8128 / auPRC 0.7682 vs. LLM-based detection 0.7376 / 0.6575
Backdoor (trigger 'cf'), 8B LoRA, SST2: UniGuardian auROC 0.9994 / auPRC 0.9995 vs. LLM-based detection 0.9591 / 0.9311
Backdoor (trigger 'cf'), 8B Full, Open Question: UniGuardian auROC 0.9910 / auPRC 0.9936
Backdoor (trigger 'I watched 3D movies'), 8B Full, Open Question: UniGuardian auROC 0.9982 / auPRC 0.9984
Adversarial, 32B (Qwen), SST2: UniGuardian auROC 0.8027 / auPRC 0.7743 vs. Granite-Guardian-3.1-8B 0.7840 / 0.7739
Adversarial, 70B (Llama), SST2: UniGuardian auROC 0.8115 / auPRC 0.7956; Emotion: 0.7716 / 0.7321
Backdoor poisoned-input suspicion scores can exceed 4,000 while clean inputs remain near/below 80
Weak baselines (Prompt-Guard-86M, Llama-Guard-3-1B/8B, OpenAI Moderation) hover near auROC 0.5000 on injection/backdoor because manipulated outputs are not explicitly harmful
baselines_compared: LLM-based detection (LLM asked yes/no whether prompt is safe)
